content:
# 2 Peter Chapter 3 — Disagreements Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Agents:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)

---

## Verse 1 — "pure mind" vs. "honest thinking"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "the pure mind of you" |
| LOGOS | "your pure mind" |
| PNEUMA | "your honest thinking" |

**Greek:** EILIKRINE DIANOIAN (pure/sincere mind/thinking)
**Issue:** PNEUMA's "honest thinking" interprets EILIKRINE dynamically. EMET and LOGOS retain "pure mind" closer to the Greek.
**Resolution:** Adopted "pure mind" (EMET/LOGOS). The Greek EILIKRINEE means unmixed, tested by sunlight -- "pure" preserves the semantic range better than "honest."

---

## Verse 3 — "mockers with mockery" vs. "scoffers with scoffing" vs. "mockers... ridiculing the truth"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "mockers with mockery" |
| LOGOS | "scoffers will come with scoffing" |
| PNEUMA | "mockers will come, living by their own desires and ridiculing the truth" |

**Greek:** EMPAIKTAI EN EMPAIGMONE (mockers in/with mockery)
**Issue:** EMET and LOGOS preserve the Greek cognate figure (noun + cognate dative). PNEUMA adds interpretive expansion ("ridiculing the truth" is not in the Greek).
**Resolution:** Adopted "mockers will come with mockery" (EMET/LOGOS blend). The cognate construction is a Semitic-style intensifier worth preserving.

---

## Verse 4 — "the fathers" vs. "our ancestors"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "the fathers" |
| LOGOS | "the fathers" |
| PNEUMA | "our ancestors" |

**Greek:** HOI PATERES (the fathers)
**Issue:** PNEUMA modernizes to "our ancestors." EMET and LOGOS retain the traditional rendering.
**Resolution:** Adopted "the fathers" (EMET/LOGOS). The term carries specific theological weight referring to the patriarchs and early generations; "ancestors" is too generic.

---

## Verse 5 — Rendering of THELONTAS (willful ignorance)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "willing [it so]" |
| LOGOS | "by their own choosing" |
| PNEUMA | "deliberately" |

**Greek:** THELONTAS — present participle of THELO (to will, to wish)
**Issue:** Three different approaches to conveying willful ignorance. EMET is most literal but awkward; PNEUMA is clear but loses the participial force; LOGOS balances both.
**Resolution:** Adopted "by their own choosing" (LOGOS). It conveys the deliberate, volitional character of the ignorance while reading naturally.

---

## Verse 5 — "having been constituted" vs. "was formed" vs. "was formed"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "having been constituted by the word of the God" |
| LOGOS | "was formed... by the word of God" |
| PNEUMA | "by God's word... the earth was formed" |

**Greek:** SUNESTOSA TO TOU THEOU LOGO (having been constituted by the word of God)
**Issue:** EMET preserves the perfect participle "having been constituted" from SUNISTEMI. LOGOS and PNEUMA simplify to "was formed."
**Resolution:** Adopted "was formed" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). While EMET's "constituted" is technically precise for SUNISTEMI, "formed" communicates the creation concept clearly and is supported by the context.

---

## Verse 6 — Sentence structure

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "through which [things] the then world, having been flooded with water, perished" |
| LOGOS | "through which the world that then existed was destroyed, being flooded with water" |
| PNEUMA | "it was by those same means that the world of that time was deluged with water and destroyed" |

**Greek:** DI HON HO TOTE KOSMOS HUDATI KATAKLUSTHEIS APOLETO
**Issue:** Clause order and participial rendering differ. EMET preserves Greek word order. LOGOS restructures for English clarity. PNEUMA paraphrases substantially.
**Resolution:** Adopted a LOGOS-based rendering with EMET influence: "through which the world that then existed, having been flooded with water, was destroyed." This preserves the participial construction while maintaining clarity.

---

## Verse 9 — "repentance" vs. "change of heart"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "repentance" |
| LOGOS | "repentance" |
| PNEUMA | "a change of heart" |

**Greek:** METANOIAN (repentance, change of mind)
**Issue:** PNEUMA renders METANOIA dynamically as "a change of heart." While etymologically defensible (META + NOIA = change of mind), "repentance" is the established theological term.
**Resolution:** Adopted "repentance" (EMET/LOGOS). This is a foundational biblical concept with a well-established English equivalent; dynamic paraphrase risks diluting its theological weight.

---

## Verse 10 — "will be found" vs. "will be found out" vs. "will be laid bare"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "will be found" |
| LOGOS | "will be found out" |
| PNEUMA | "will be laid bare" |

**Greek:** HEURETHESETAI (will be found) — Sinaiticus reading; later MSS read KATAKAESETAI (will be burned up)
**Issue:** This is one of the most significant textual variants in 2 Peter. All three agents correctly followed the Sinaiticus reading rather than the later Byzantine variant. The disagreement is over how to render HEURETHESETAI in context. EMET gives the bare lexical meaning. LOGOS adds the idiomatic nuance of discovery/exposure. PNEUMA interprets more freely.
**Resolution:** Adopted "will be found out" (LOGOS). This preserves the verb HEURISKO while conveying the sense of exposure and divine scrutiny implied by the context. PNEUMA's "laid bare" is too interpretive; EMET's bare "will be found" may confuse English readers.

---

## Verse 10 — "with a rushing sound" vs. "with a rushing noise" vs. "with a tremendous roar"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "with a rushing sound" |
| LOGOS | "with a rushing noise" |
| PNEUMA | "with a tremendous roar" |

**Greek:** HROIZEDON (with a rushing/whizzing sound)
**Issue:** PNEUMA amplifies the sound imagery ("tremendous roar") beyond what the Greek adverb warrants. EMET and LOGOS are closer to the lexical meaning.
**Resolution:** Adopted "with a rushing noise" (LOGOS). The Greek adverb HROIZEDON denotes a whizzing or rushing sound; "tremendous roar" overstates the term.

---

## Verse 11 — Sentence construction (question vs. statement)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "what sort [of persons] ought you to be in holy conduct and godliness" |
| LOGOS | "what sort of persons ought you to be in holy conduct and godliness" |
| PNEUMA | "what kind of people should you be? You should live holy and godly lives" |

**Greek:** POTAPOUS DEI HUPARCHEIN HUMAS EN HAGIAIS ANASTROPHAIS KAI EUSEBEIAIS
**Issue:** PNEUMA splits this into a question and answer, adding "You should live holy and godly lives" as an explicit answer. EMET and LOGOS treat the whole clause as a rhetorical question that implies its own answer.
**Resolution:** Adopted the rhetorical question form (EMET/LOGOS). The Greek is a single rhetorical question; splitting it into Q&A adds text not present in the source.

---

## Verse 12 — "expecting and hastening" vs. "looking for and hastening" vs. "eagerly waiting for and hastening"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "expecting and hastening" |
| LOGOS | "looking for and hastening" |
| PNEUMA | "eagerly waiting for and hastening the arrival" |

**Greek:** PROSDOKONTAS KAI SPEUDONTAS (expecting/looking for and hastening)
**Issue:** Minor variation. PNEUMA adds "eagerly" and "arrival" which are not in the immediate Greek text.
**Resolution:** Adopted "looking for and hastening" (LOGOS). Clean, accurate, preserves both participles without addition.

---

## Verse 13 — "righteousness dwells" vs. "righteousness is at home"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "righteousness dwells" |
| LOGOS | "righteousness dwells" |
| PNEUMA | "righteousness is at home" |

**Greek:** DIKAIOSUNE KATOIKEI (righteousness dwells/settles)
**Issue:** PNEUMA's "is at home" is a creative rendering of KATOIKEI that captures the permanence implied by the verb (KATA + OIKEO = to dwell down, settle permanently). However, "dwells" adequately conveys this.
**Resolution:** Adopted "righteousness dwells" (EMET/LOGOS). While PNEUMA's rendering is insightful, "dwells" is the standard translation and carries sufficient weight. The permanence is noted in the PNEUMA meaning notes.

---

## Verse 14 — Word order: "spotless and unblemished in peace" vs. "in peace, spotless and blameless"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "spotless and unblemished in peace" |
| LOGOS | "in peace, spotless and blameless" |
| PNEUMA | "at peace with God -- spotless and without fault" |

**Greek:** ASPILOUS KAI AMOMETOUS AUTO EN EIRENE HEURETHEENAI (spotless and blameless by him in peace to be found)
**Issue:** The placement of EN EIRENE (in peace) and the relationship of the adjectives to the verb HEURETHEENAI are rendered differently. PNEUMA adds "with God" which is not in the text.
**Resolution:** Adopted "in peace, spotless and blameless" (LOGOS). The placement of "in peace" before the adjectives reads naturally and avoids PNEUMA's interpretive addition.

---

## Verse 15 — "Paulos" vs. "Paul"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "Paulos" |
| LOGOS | "Paul" |
| PNEUMA | "Paul" |

**Greek:** PAULOS
**Issue:** EMET transliterates the Greek form; LOGOS and PNEUMA use the conventional English form.
**Resolution:** Adopted "Paul" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). The SOPHIA Bible uses standard English name forms throughout, consistent with prior consensus decisions.

---

## Verse 15 — "consider as salvation" vs. "regard... as salvation" vs. "regard... as an opportunity for salvation"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "[consider as] salvation" |
| LOGOS | "regard... as salvation" |
| PNEUMA | "regard... as an opportunity for salvation" |

**Greek:** TEEN TOU KURIOU HEMON MAKROTHUMIAN SOTERIAN HEEGEISTHE (the patience of our Lord as salvation consider)
**Issue:** PNEUMA adds "an opportunity for" which is not in the Greek. The text straightforwardly equates divine patience with salvation, not merely an opportunity for it.
**Resolution:** Adopted "regard... as salvation" (LOGOS). The Greek directly identifies patience as salvation; adding "opportunity for" weakens the equation.

---

## Verse 16 — "unlearned" vs. "ignorant"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "unlearned" |
| LOGOS | "unlearned" |
| PNEUMA | "ignorant" |

**Greek:** AMATHEIS (unlearned, ignorant)
**Issue:** Minor lexical choice. Both are valid renderings of AMATHEIS.
**Resolution:** Adopted "unlearned" (EMET/LOGOS). It is closer to the Greek etymology (A + MATHEIN = un-learned) and slightly less pejorative in modern English than "ignorant."

---

## Verse 17 — "firmness" vs. "steadfastness" vs. "firm footing"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "firmness" |
| LOGOS | "steadfastness" |
| PNEUMA | "firm footing" |

**Greek:** STERIGMOU (firmness, stability, steadfastness)
**Issue:** Three approaches to STERIGMOS. EMET is most literal; LOGOS uses a standard theological term; PNEUMA employs a physical metaphor.
**Resolution:** Adopted "steadfastness" (LOGOS). It captures both the stability and the perseverance dimensions of STERIGMOS and is the most established English equivalent.

---

## Verse 18 — "Yeshua Christos" vs. "Jesus Christ"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "Yeshua Christos" |
| LOGOS | "Jesus Christ" |
| PNEUMA | "Jesus Christ" |

**Greek:** IESOU CHRISTOU
**Issue:** EMET uses Hebrew/Greek transliterations; LOGOS and PNEUMA use standard English forms.
**Resolution:** Adopted "Jesus Christ" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). Consistent with the SOPHIA Bible's use of conventional English name forms.

---

## Summary Statistics

| Category | Count |
|----------|-------|
| Total disagreements logged | 18 |
| Resolved in favor of EMET (literal) | 1 |
| Resolved in favor of LOGOS (formal) | 12 |
| Resolved in favor of PNEUMA (dynamic) | 0 |
| Resolved as EMET/LOGOS blend | 4 |
| Resolved as three-way blend | 1 |

**Observation:** 2 Peter 3 showed unusually strong convergence toward LOGOS renderings. The formal equivalence approach was particularly well-suited to this chapter's dense theological argumentation and eschatological language, where precision matters and dynamic paraphrase risks over-interpretation. PNEUMA's distinctive contributions (especially "laid bare" in v. 10 and "is at home" in v. 13) were noted as valuable interpretive insights even when not adopted for the consensus text.
